chapter: Chapter 12: Earth, Moon, and the Sun

questions.question: Why does a lunar eclipse not occur every full moon?
questions.answer: Because the Moon’s orbit is tilted about 5° to the ecliptic plane, so alignment with Earth’s shadow happens only occasionally.

questions.question: Explain why the apparent size of the Sun and Moon appear nearly equal during a total solar eclipse.
questions.answer: The Sun is much larger but farther away, while the Moon is smaller but closer; their apparent sizes in the sky coincide from Earth.

questions.question: Why is Earth’s equatorial diameter slightly larger than its polar diameter?
questions.answer: Because Earth rotates, causing a bulging at the equator due to centrifugal force.

questions.question: How does the Moon’s gravitational pull create bulges in Earth’s oceans?
questions.answer: The Moon’s gravity pulls water toward it, creating a tidal bulge, while centrifugal force creates another on the opposite side.

questions.question: Why does the duration of a solar eclipse vary across different locations?
questions.answer: Because the Moon’s shadow moves across Earth’s surface and its speed and width affect how long the eclipse is visible at each location.

questions.question: Explain why the Moon shows the same face to Earth at all times.
questions.answer: Because it is tidally locked, rotating once on its axis in the same time it takes to orbit Earth.

questions.question: Why is a solar day slightly longer than a sidereal day?
questions.answer: Because Earth moves along its orbit, requiring extra rotation for the Sun to return to the same position in the sky.

questions.question: Why do spring tides occur twice a month?
questions.answer: They occur during new and full moons when Sun, Moon, and Earth are aligned, enhancing gravitational pull on oceans.

questions.question: Explain the effect of Earth’s elliptical orbit on seasons.
questions.answer: Although seasons are mostly due to axial tilt, the elliptical orbit slightly affects the length of seasons due to varying orbital speed.

questions.question: Why does the Moon appear slightly larger near the horizon than at zenith?
questions.answer: Due to the Moon illusion, a psychological effect, not an actual size change.

questions.question: Why is the total duration of a lunar eclipse longer than a total solar eclipse?
questions.answer: Because Earth’s shadow is much larger than the Moon’s, taking longer to pass over the Moon.

questions.question: How does libration allow us to see more than half of the Moon’s surface over time?
questions.answer: Libration results from the Moon’s orbital eccentricity and tilt, causing slight oscillations in apparent position.

questions.question: Why is the inclination of the Moon’s orbit important for eclipse prediction?
questions.answer: Because eclipses only occur when the Moon is near the orbital nodes where it crosses the ecliptic plane.

questions.question: Explain the difference between the anomalistic month and the sidereal month.
questions.answer: An anomalistic month is the time between successive perigees, while a sidereal month is the time for the Moon to orbit Earth relative to the stars.

questions.question: Why is the path of totality in a solar eclipse so narrow?
questions.answer: Because the Moon’s umbra cone is small, covering only a narrow strip on Earth’s surface.

questions.question: How does the Moon’s gravity influence Earth’s rotation?
questions.answer: Tidal friction from Moon’s gravity gradually slows Earth’s rotation, lengthening days over time.

questions.question: Why does the Moon appear red during a total lunar eclipse?
questions.answer: Earth’s atmosphere scatters blue light and refracts red light onto the Moon’s surface.

questions.question: Explain why the Earth experiences two high tides and two low tides daily.
questions.answer: Because the Moon’s gravitational pull creates bulges on opposite sides, and Earth’s rotation causes two cycles per day.

questions.question: Why is the Moon’s apparent motion in the sky eastward relative to stars?
questions.answer: Because the Moon orbits Earth from west to east, moving relative to the background stars.

questions.question: Why do solar eclipses last for only a few minutes at any location?
questions.answer: Because the Moon’s shadow moves quickly across Earth and the umbra covers only a small area.

questions.question: How does Earth’s axial precession affect the timing of seasons over millennia?
questions.answer: Precession gradually shifts the orientation of Earth’s axis, altering the timing of equinoxes and solstices over long periods.

questions.question: Explain why the Moon has no atmosphere to scatter sunlight during eclipses.
questions.answer: The Moon’s gravity is too weak to retain an atmosphere, so there is no scattering of sunlight locally.

questions.question: Why does the Earth-Moon system rotate around a common center of mass (barycenter)?
questions.answer: Because both exert gravitational forces on each other; the barycenter lies inside Earth but not at its center.

questions.question: How does the inclination of Earth’s axis influence the Arctic and Antarctic circles?
questions.answer: It determines the latitudes where polar day and polar night occur, creating the Arctic and Antarctic circles at 66.5° N and S.

questions.question: Explain why predicting exact tidal heights requires both Sun and Moon positions.
questions.answer: Because tides result from the combined gravitational forces of Sun and Moon, and their alignment affects height and timing.
